text: well known to everyone. Mauss quite simply says that the book is built on a truism, offering nothing new. Third, Mauss accuses the book of a generic defect (which the Durkheimians often pointed out, especially with reference to the British school), namely that of presenting a myriad of ethnographic and historical facts instead of bringing analysis to bear on a circumscribed and thoroughly studied case (Mauss actually comes close to ridiculing the book as ‘une randonnée à travers l’histoire et l’ethnographie’, i.e. a loose narrative jumping randomly around in ethnographic and historical facts). These are without much doubt the least convincing pages that Marcel Mauss ever put his name to. They are also close to embarrassing, and for some quite evident reasons, pertaining to each of the three points of the critique. First, van Gennep is explicitly not proposing any ‘law’. ‘Law’ is not a word that van Gennep even uses in Rites of Passage – whereas it was indeed typical of Durkheim and his followers. 10 The word that van Gennep does use is that of ‘schema’, which quite evidently must be related to shared forms and ‘rhythms’, to patterns that are indeed comparable at the general level, but that do not translate easily into law-like statements concerning ‘causes’ or ‘functions’ of rites. Mauss simply misreads and distorts the entire argument by forcing van Gennep’s analytical effort into his own Durkheimian mould of what ‘theorizing’ may mean – actually here exposing the inherent problem with Durkheimian neo-Kantianism, 10 One year later, in one of his follow-up books, La Formation des légendes, van Gennep actually replaces the earlier employed term ‘schema’ with ‘law’, but openly expresses his own regret at ‘having to use’ this highly problematic term, uncritically borrowed from the natural sciences. Van Gennep justifies his choice, and not without irony, by referring to the fact that ‘some other ethnographers and folklorists have not felt the same scruple’ (in Belmont 1979: 45). Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 61 more so than managing to point out van Gennep’s ‘shortcomings’. Still on this first point of critique, that van Gennep ‘sees rites of passage everywhere’ and extends that ‘law’ beyond the analytically meaningful, it is in fact quite striking what Mauss is actually blaming van Gennep for: van Gennep indicates no aim whatsoever to ground a ‘theory of thought and knowledge’ from the structure of rites of passage. He quotes Nietzsche once (shame on you!), but this is simply with reference to Nietzsche’s notion of the eternal recurrence which seems to resonate well with beliefs among many peoples (he cites the Lushae) who see life itself as circular. 11 It is true that van Gennep’s approach does open up the possibility of an entirely different ‘foundation’ of society, and arguably even of conceptual thought: namely in human experiences of, and responses to, liminality. Rather than following Durkheimian dogmatism, such a theory of foundation would have had to move completely outside a (neo-)Kantian framework, and not simply solve the problem as posed from within it. To some extent, it was this idea that Victor Turner became engaged with later in his career when he encountered the work of Dilthey: the relationship between thought and experience in liminal moments. We shall have a chance to return to this discussion. But van Gennep? Van Gennep had made no such claim about presenting a theory of knowledge. So where does this come from? Well, it probably comes from Durkheim. In his Introduction to EFRL, Durkheim famously made the hubristic claim to have solved the philosophical problem of knowledge. By grounding Kant’s a priori in social facts, in social divisions and categories, Durkheim claimed to have established not only the origin of society and religion, but equally the explanation of how we can know things at all. Durkheim was working on that theory in that very period. So instead of detecting a potential point from which to revise their own positions concerning cognition and thought, what Mauss does is to inject van Gennep’s book with an ambition it simply does not express, a straw-man which he then duly demolishes. Second, it may well be argued that in one sense Rites of Passage is a truism. However, and as Mauss again fails to recognize, that is exactly what van Gennep himself claims: what he argues is not a deduction or even an analytical extrapolation, and he is expressly not proposing any division or classification which is not already present in the material itself. 12 Most genuine human and scientific discoveries possess this ‘A-ha’ nature: but of course! We see something 11 Van Gennep rarely if ever made analytical ‘leaps’ to more purely philosophical debates, always arguing from the ground of available data. The only philosopher van Gennep mentions in Rites of Passage is in fact Nietzsche, in the second-to-last paragraph of the book. This is where van Gennep compares rectilinear to cyclical patterns, and notes how the circular order from life to death and death to life (the sequential order of the book itself) acquired a ‘psychological significance’ in Nietzsche’s theory of eternal recurrence (1960: 194). 12 It is striking how Mauss even on this point will later start to emulate van Gennep. In his crucial 1927 publication, where Mauss proposes a new division of sociology and its subject fields, he justifies the proposed division as follows: ‘The division is clear and distinct. It does not divide anything which is not perfectly divided in reality’ (Mauss 2005: 60). Liminality and the Modern 62 which was there, but which we previously just could not see – obvious at it was, yet not fully visible; something which we just could not pin down with precise words. The related point to stress is that this was indeed, at the very same time, fundamentally new. No-one had said it before, and no-one has ever since said it so clearly and so convincingly. Mauss was quite plainly wrong about his assessment. Van Gennep’s classificatory effort has stood the test of time.
